code: bbfm_out <- extract(bbf_object, "model")$BBFM  body_object <- methods::new("FLS",   metadata = list(ID = "body"),   model_parameters = list(),   model = list(),   body = extract(bbf_object, "body"),   shape_parameters = extract(bbf_object, c("shape_parameters", "body")) )  body_object <- target_strength(   object = body_object,   frequency = bbfm_out$frequency,   model = "dwba",   density_sw = 1026.8,   sound_speed_sw = 1477.3 )  backbone_object <- methods::new("FLS",   metadata = list(ID = "backbone"),   model_parameters = list(),   model = list(),   body = extract(bbf_object, "backbone"),   shape_parameters = extract(bbf_object, c("shape_parameters", "backbone")) )  backbone_object <- target_strength(   object = backbone_object,   frequency = bbfm_out$frequency,   model = "ecms",   density_sw = 1026.8,   sound_speed_sw = 1477.3,   density_body = extract(bbf_object, c("backbone", "density")),   sound_speed_longitudinal_body = extract(     bbf_object,     c("backbone", "sound_speed_longitudinal")   ),   sound_speed_transversal_body = extract(     bbf_object,     c("backbone", "sound_speed_transversal")   ) )  backbone_body <- extract(backbone_object, "body") x_center <- mean(range(backbone_body$rpos["x", ], na.rm = TRUE)) z_center <- mean(range(backbone_body$rpos["z", ], na.rm = TRUE)) phase_shift <- exp(   2i * acousticTS::wavenumber(bbfm_out$frequency, 1477.3) *     (x_center * cos(backbone_body$theta) + z_center * sin(backbone_body$theta)) )  reconstructed_fbs <-   extract(body_object, "model")$DWBA$f_bs +   extract(backbone_object, "model")$ECMS$f_bs * phase_shift  bbfm_check <- data.frame(   frequency_kHz = bbfm_out$frequency * 1e-3,   delta_f_bs = Mod(bbfm_out$f_bs - reconstructed_fbs),   delta_TS_dB = bbfm_out$TS - 10 * log10(abs(reconstructed_fbs)^2) )  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     quantity = c("Max $|\\Delta f_{bs}|$", "Max $|\\Delta TS|$ (dB)"),     value = c(       max(bbfm_check$delta_f_bs),       max(abs(bbfm_check$delta_TS_dB))     )   ),   digits = 6 )

code: library(acousticTS)  density_sw <- 1026.8 sound_speed_sw <- 1477.3  straight_shape <- cylinder(   length_body = 10.5e-3,   radius_body = 1e-3,   n_segments = 401 )  straight_object <- fls_generate(   shape = straight_shape,   density_body = density_sw * 1.0357,   sound_speed_body = sound_speed_sw * 1.0279,   theta_body = pi / 2 )  bent_object <- brake(   straight_object,   radius_curvature = 1.5 )  straight_object ## FLS-object ##  Fluid-like scatterer  ##  ID:UID ## Body dimensions: ##  Length:0.011 m(n = 401 cylinders) ##  Mean radius:0.001 m ##  Max radius:0.001 m ## Shape parameters: ##  Defined shape:Cylinder ##  L/a ratio:10.5 ##  Taper order:N/A ## Material properties: ##  Density: 1063.4568 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 1518.5167 m s^-1 ## Body orientation (relative to transducer face/axis):1.571 radians bent_object ## FLS-object ##  Fluid-like scatterer  ##  ID:UID ## Body dimensions: ##  Length:0.01 m(n = 401 cylinders) ##  Mean radius:0.001 m ##  Max radius:0.001 m ## Shape parameters: ##  Defined shape:Cylinder ##  L/a ratio:10.5 ##  Taper order:N/A ## Material properties: ##  Density: 1063.4568 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 1518.5167 m s^-1 ## Body orientation (relative to transducer face/axis):1.571 radians

code: library(acousticTS)  shape_obj <- prolate_spheroid(   length_body = 0.04,   radius_body = 0.004,   n_segments = 60 )  fls_obj <- fls_generate(   shape = shape_obj,   density_body = 1045,   sound_speed_body = 1520 )  gas_obj <- gas_generate(   shape = sphere(radius_body = 0.01, n_segments = 60),   g_fluid = 0.0012,   h_fluid = 0.22 )  data.frame(   object_class = c(class(fls_obj)[1], class(gas_obj)[1]),   shape = c(     extract(fls_obj, c("shape_parameters", "shape")),     extract(gas_obj, c("shape_parameters", "shape"))   ),   length_m = c(     extract(fls_obj, c("shape_parameters", "length")),     extract(gas_obj, c("shape_parameters", "length"))   ) ) ##   object_class           shape length_m ## 1          FLS ProlateSpheroid     0.04 ## 2          GAS          Sphere     0.02 old_par <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) on.exit(par(old_par), add = TRUE)  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(3, 3, 2.2, 0.8)) plot(fls_obj, type = "shape", main = "FLS object") plot(gas_obj, type = "shape", main = "GAS object")

code: # Explicit body-plus-backbone target for BBFM workflows bbf_obj <- bbf_generate(   body_shape = arbitrary(     x_body = c(0, 0.04, 0.08),     zU_body = c(0.001, 0.004, 0.001),     zL_body = c(-0.001, -0.004, -0.001)   ),   backbone_shape = cylinder(     length_body = 0.05,     radius_body = 0.0008,     n_segments = 40   ),   density_body = 1070,   sound_speed_body = 1570,   density_backbone = 1900,   sound_speed_longitudinal_backbone = 3500,   sound_speed_transversal_backbone = 1700 )  # Standard calibration sphere cal_obj <- cal_generate(   material = "WC",   diameter = 38.1e-3,   n_segments = 120 )  # Elastic-shelled sphere ess_obj <- ess_generate(   shape = sphere(radius_body = 0.03, n_segments = 80),   shell_thickness = 0.001,   density_shell = 1050,   sound_speed_shell = 2350,   density_fluid = 1030,   sound_speed_fluid = 1500,   E = 3.5e9,   nu = 0.34 ) cal_obj <- cal_generate(   material = "WC",   diameter = 38.1e-3,   n_segments = 120 )  ess_obj <- ess_generate(   shape = sphere(radius_body = 0.03, n_segments = 80),   shell_thickness = 0.001,   density_shell = 1050,   sound_speed_shell = 2350,   density_fluid = 1030,   sound_speed_fluid = 1500,   E = 3.5e9,   nu = 0.34 )  data.frame(   object_class = c(class(cal_obj)[1], class(ess_obj)[1]),   diameter_m = c(     extract(cal_obj, c("shape_parameters", "diameter")),     NA_real_   ),   shell_thickness_m = c(     NA_real_,     extract(ess_obj, c("shell", "shell_thickness"))   ) ) ##   object_class diameter_m shell_thickness_m ## 1          CAL     0.0381                NA ## 2          ESS         NA             0.001

title: Building shapes
text: getting interpretation tradeoffs, helps see main shape builders direct use. four examples cover main canonical branches. important practical point constructor returns Shape object can later passed scatterer constructor without re-entering geometry. usually worth plotting objects immediately stored geometry checked, just assumed constructor call.
code: library(acousticTS)  sphere_shape <- sphere(radius_body = 0.01, n_segments = 40) cylinder_shape <- cylinder(length_body = 0.05, radius_body = 0.003, n_segments = 80) ps_shape <- prolate_spheroid(length_body = 0.04, radius_body = 0.004, n_segments = 60) oblate_shape <- oblate_spheroid(length_body = 0.012, radius_body = 0.01, n_segments = 60)  shape_summary <- function(shape_obj) {   params <- extract(shape_obj, "shape_parameters")   pos <- extract(shape_obj, "position_matrix")   radius_value <- params$radius   if (length(radius_value) > 1) {     radius_value <- max(radius_value, na.rm = TRUE)   }    data.frame(     shape = class(shape_obj)[1],     length_m = max(pos[, 1], na.rm = TRUE) - min(pos[, 1], na.rm = TRUE),     max_radius_m = radius_value,     n_segments = params$n_segments   ) }  do.call(   rbind,   lapply(     list(sphere_shape, cylinder_shape, ps_shape, oblate_shape),     shape_summary   ) ) ##             shape length_m max_radius_m n_segments ## 1          Sphere    0.020        0.010         40 ## 2        Cylinder    0.050        0.003         80 ## 3 ProlateSpheroid    0.040        0.004         60 ## 4  OblateSpheroid    0.012        0.010         60 plot_shape_profile <- function(shape_obj, main) {   pos <- extract(shape_obj, "position_matrix")   x <- pos[, "x"]   zU <- pos[, "zU"]   zL <- pos[, "zL"]   ylim <- range(c(zU, zL), finite = TRUE)    plot(x, zU,     type = "l",     ylim = ylim,     xlab = "x",     ylab = "z",     main = main   )   lines(x, zL)   lines(x, rep(0, length(x)), lty = 3, col = "grey70") }  old_par <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) on.exit(par(old_par), add = TRUE)  par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(3, 3, 2.2, 0.8)) plot_shape_profile(sphere_shape, main = "Sphere") plot_shape_profile(cylinder_shape, main = "Cylinder") plot_shape_profile(ps_shape, main = "Prolate spheroid") plot_shape_profile(oblate_shape, main = "Oblate spheroid")

code: tsl_initialize <- function(object,                            frequency,                            intercept = -70,                            slope = 20) {   shape <- acousticTS::extract(object, "shape_parameters")    if (is.null(shape$length) || is.na(shape$length)) {     stop(       "TSL requires the target shape to have a defined length."     )   }    model_params <- list(     parameters = data.frame(       frequency = frequency     ),     body = data.frame(       length_m = shape$length     ),     coefficients = data.frame(       intercept = intercept,       slope = slope     )   )    methods::slot(object, "model_parameters")$TSL <- model_params    methods::slot(object, "model")$TSL <- data.frame(     frequency = frequency,     sigma_bs = rep(NA_real_, length(frequency))   )    object }

title: Creating a Model from Scratch
text: model function simple. pulls prepared parameters $TSL, computes TS, converts sigma_bs, stores finished results back model slot. enough solver . make model callable target_strength(), register : point target_strength(..., model = "tsl") target_strength(..., model = "toy_tsl") resolve registered model. example repeats TS value across requested frequencies toy model use frequency. acceptable pedagogical example. frequency-dependent model instead calculate vector whose length matches input frequency vector.
code: TSL <- function(object) {   model <- acousticTS::extract(object, "model_parameters")$TSL    length_mm <- model$body$length_m * 1e3   intercept <- model$coefficients$intercept   slope <- model$coefficients$slope    TS <- intercept + slope * log10(length_mm)   sigma_bs <- acousticTS::linear(TS)    methods::slot(object, "model")$TSL <- data.frame(     frequency = model$parameters$frequency,     f_bs = rep(sqrt(sigma_bs), nrow(model$parameters)),     sigma_bs = rep(sigma_bs, nrow(model$parameters)),     TS = rep(TS, nrow(model$parameters))   )    object } register_model(   name = "tsl",   initialize = tsl_initialize,   solver = TSL,   slot = "TSL",   aliases = "toy_tsl" )

code: precision_freq <- c(12e3, 18e3, 38e3, 70e3, 100e3) precision_obj <- fls_generate(   shape = prolate_spheroid(     length_body = 0.14,     radius_body = 0.01,     n_segments = 80   ),   theta_body = pi / 2,   density_body = 1028.9,   sound_speed_body = 1480.3 )  precision_double <- target_strength(   object = precision_obj,   frequency = precision_freq,   model = "psms",   boundary = "liquid_filled",   phi_body = pi,   adaptive = FALSE,   precision = "double",   simplify_Amn = FALSE,   n_integration = 96,   density_sw = 1026.8,   sound_speed_sw = 1477.3 )  precision_quad <- target_strength(   object = precision_obj,   frequency = precision_freq,   model = "psms",   boundary = "liquid_filled",   phi_body = pi,   adaptive = FALSE,   precision = "quad",   simplify_Amn = FALSE,   n_integration = 96,   density_sw = 1026.8,   sound_speed_sw = 1477.3 )  precision_df <- data.frame(   frequency = precision_freq,   k0b = 2 * pi * precision_freq * 0.01 / 1477.3,   double_ts = precision_double@model$PSMS$TS,   quad_ts = precision_quad@model$PSMS$TS ) precision_df$delta_ts <- precision_df$double_ts - precision_df$quad_ts

title: Prolate spheroidal modal series solution (PSMS) theory
text: Assume linear acoustics harmonic time dependence e^{-\omega t}. homogeneous region, pressure satisfies Helmholtz equation. scattering problem proceeds three steps. First, incident field expanded regular spheroidal eigenfunctions. Second, scattered field expanded outgoing spheroidal eigenfunctions. Third, spheroid contains interior fluid gas, interior field expanded regular interior spheroidal eigenfunctions. boundary conditions determine modal coefficients. summary simple, already contains main distinction rigid pressure-release cases interior-fluid cases. exterior basis matters, coefficient bookkeeping remains effectively local. interior medium different reduced frequency introduced, basis mismatch becomes part physics part algebra.

title: Prolate spheroidal modal series solution (PSMS) theory
text: Let q denote semifocal length spheroid, let (\xi,\eta,\phi) prolate spheroidal coordinates. Cartesian coordinates: x = q\sqrt{(\xi^2-1)(1-\eta^2)}\cos\phi,   \qquad   y = q\sqrt{(\xi^2-1)(1-\eta^2)}\sin\phi,   \qquad   z = q\xi\eta. coordinate ranges : \xi \ge 1,   \qquad -1 \le \eta \le 1,   \qquad 0 \le \phi < 2\pi. code: shape_obj <- cylinder(   length_body = 0.05,   radius_body = 0.003,   n_segments = 120 )  obj <- fls_generate(   shape = shape_obj,   density_body = 1045,   sound_speed_body = 1520 )  bent_ratio <- brake(obj, radius_curvature = 5, mode = "ratio") bent_measured <- brake(obj, radius_curvature = 0.35, mode = "measurement")  curvature_ratio <- function(x) {   value <- extract(x, "shape_parameters")$radius_curvature_ratio   if (is.null(value)) NA_real_ else value }  data.frame(   object = c("original", "bent_ratio", "bent_measured"),   length_m = c(     extract(obj, c("shape_parameters", "length")),     extract(bent_ratio, c("shape_parameters", "length")),     extract(bent_measured, c("shape_parameters", "length"))   ),   curvature_ratio = c(     curvature_ratio(obj),     curvature_ratio(bent_ratio),     curvature_ratio(bent_measured)   ) ) ##          object   length_m curvature_ratio ## 1      original 0.05000000              NA ## 2    bent_ratio 0.04999999               5 ## 3 bent_measured 0.05000000               7 old_par <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) on.exit(par(old_par))  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(3.2, 3.2, 2.6, 0.8)) plot(obj, type = "shape", main = "Original FLS cylinder") plot(bent_ratio, type = "shape", main = "After brake(mode = \"ratio\")") body_before <- extract(obj, "body")$rpos body_after <- extract(bent_ratio, "body")$rpos stations <- unique(round(seq(1, ncol(body_before), length.out = 5)))  data.frame(   station = stations,   x_before = round(body_before["x", stations], 5),   z_before = round(body_before["z", stations], 5),   x_after = round(body_after["x", stations], 5),   z_after = round(body_after["z", stations], 5) ) ##   station x_before z_before x_after  z_after ## 1       1   0.0500        0 0.04996 -0.00125 ## 2      31   0.0375        0 0.03749 -0.00031 ## 3      61   0.0250        0 0.02500  0.00000 ## 4      91   0.0125        0 0.01251 -0.00031 ## 5     121   0.0000        0 0.00004 -0.00125 centerline_arc_length <- function(x) {   body <- extract(x, "body")   rpos <- body$rpos   sum(sqrt(diff(rpos["x", ])^2 + diff(rpos["z", ])^2)) }  bent_rescaled <- reforge(bent_ratio, body_target = c(length = 0.08)) body_bent_rescaled <- extract(bent_rescaled, "body")$rpos  data.frame(   metric = c(     "target_length_m",     "stored_shape_length_m",     "centerline_arc_length_m",     "projected_x_length_m"   ),   value = c(     0.08,     extract(bent_rescaled, c("shape_parameters", "length")),     centerline_arc_length(bent_rescaled),     diff(range(body_bent_rescaled["x", ]))   ) ) ##                    metric      value ## 1         target_length_m 0.08000000 ## 2   stored_shape_length_m 0.08000000 ## 3 centerline_arc_length_m 0.08000000 ## 4    projected_x_length_m 0.07986674

code: translated_obj <- translate_shape(obj, x_offset = -0.025) centered_obj <- reanchor_shape(obj, anchor = "center", at = 0)  data.frame(   object = c("original", "translated", "centered"),   x_min = c(     min(extract(obj, "body")$rpos["x", ]),     min(extract(translated_obj, "body")$rpos["x", ]),     min(extract(centered_obj, "body")$rpos["x", ])   ),   x_max = c(     max(extract(obj, "body")$rpos["x", ]),     max(extract(translated_obj, "body")$rpos["x", ]),     max(extract(centered_obj, "body")$rpos["x", ])   ) ) ##       object  x_min x_max ## 1   original  0.000 0.050 ## 2 translated -0.025 0.025 ## 3   centered -0.025 0.025 obj_inflated <- inflate_shape(   obj,   x_range = c(0.015, 0.035),   scale = 1.35 ) obj_smoothed <- smooth_shape(obj_inflated, span = 7) obj_dense <- resample_shape(obj_smoothed, n_segments = 120) obj_flipped <- flip_shape(obj_inflated, axis = "x")  data.frame(   object = c("original", "inflated", "dense"),   shape_label = c(     extract(obj, c("shape_parameters", "shape")),     extract(obj_inflated, c("shape_parameters", "shape")),     extract(obj_dense, c("shape_parameters", "shape"))   ),   n_segments = c(     extract(obj, c("shape_parameters", "n_segments")),     extract(obj_inflated, c("shape_parameters", "n_segments")),     extract(obj_dense, c("shape_parameters", "n_segments"))   ) ) ##     object shape_label n_segments ## 1 original    Cylinder        120 ## 2 inflated   Arbitrary        120 ## 3    dense   Arbitrary        120

## ==================================== res_fls_reforge <- simulate_ts(   object = obj,   frequency = 120e3,   model = "DWBA",   n_realizations = 2,   parameters = list(     body_target = list(       c(length = 0.04, radius = 0.0025),       c(length = 0.06, radius = 0.0035)     ),     isometric_body = FALSE,     theta_body = function() runif(1, min = 0.5 * pi, max = pi)   ),   batch_by = "body_target",   parallel = FALSE ) ## ==================================== ## Scatterer-class: FLS  ## Model(s): DWBA  ## Batching parameter(s): body_target  ## Simulated parameters: body_target, isometric_body, theta_body  ## Total simulation realizations: 4  ## Parallelize TS calculations: FALSE  ## ==================================== ## ==================================== ## Preparing sequential simulations ## ==================================== ## Simulations complete! ## ==================================== head(res_fls_reforge$DWBA[, c("body_target", "theta_body", "TS")]) ##    body_target theta_body        TS ## 1 0.04, 0.0025   2.490944 -68.15085 ## 2 0.04, 0.0025   2.235577 -68.15085 ## 3 0.06, 0.0035   2.730156 -72.25574 ## 4 0.06, 0.0035   1.845710 -72.25574 obj_bent <- brake(obj, radius_curvature = 5, mode = "ratio")  res_bent <- simulate_ts(   object = obj_bent,   frequency = 120e3,   model = "DWBA",   n_realizations = 2,   parameters = list(     length_body = c(0.04, 0.05),     theta_body = 0.5 * pi   ),   batch_by = "length_body",   parallel = FALSE ) ## ==================================== ## Scatterer-class: FLS  ## Model(s): DWBA  ## Batching parameter(s): length_body  ## Simulated parameters: length_body, theta_body  ## Total simulation realizations: 4  ## Parallelize TS calculations: FALSE  ## ==================================== ## ==================================== ## Preparing sequential simulations ## ==================================== ## Simulations complete! what: Helmholtz equation in the surrounding fluid
title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: homogeneous inviscid fluid, time-harmonic acoustic pressure satisfies: \nabla^2 p + k^2 p = 0, k = \omega/c. spherical coordinates (r,\theta,\varphi), Laplacian becomes: \nabla^2 p =     \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}   \left(r^2\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}\right) +     \frac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}   \left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial p}{\partial \theta}\right) +     \frac{1}{r^2\sin^2\theta}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial \varphi^2}. axisymmetric incidence, field independent \varphi, last term vanishes.

previous_headings: Reduction of the wave equation in spherical coordinates
title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: axisymmetric pressure can expressed product radial angular factors: p(r,\theta) = R(r)\Theta(\theta), R(r) represents radial dependence pressure field \Theta(\theta) represents angular dependence. Substituting product Helmholtz equation separating radial angular dependence gives: \frac{1}{R}\frac{d}{dr}   \left(r^2\frac{dR}{dr}\right) + k^2r^2 = -\frac{1}{\Theta\sin\theta}   \frac{d}{d\theta}\left(\sin\theta\frac{d\Theta}{d\theta}\right). left-hand side depends r right-hand side depends \theta, sides must equal separation constant. Writing constant n(n+1) gives angular equation: \frac{1}{\sin\theta}   \frac{d}{d\theta}   \left(\sin\theta\frac{d\Theta}{d\theta}\right) +     n(n+1)\Theta = 0, whose regular solutions Legendre polynomials: \Theta(\theta) = P_n(\cos\theta). radial equation takes form: \frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2\frac{dR}{dr}\right) +     \left(k^2r^2 - n(n+1)\right)R = 0, whose independent solutions spherical Bessel, oeumann, Hankel functions. essential point spherical geometry keeps angular order n separated exactly. partial wave can solved independently boundary condition imposed.

title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: angular modes decouple Legendre polynomials satisfy: \int_{-1}^{1} P_m(\mu)P_n(\mu)\,d\mu =     \frac{2}{2n+1}\delta_{mn}. orthogonality key step turns continuous boundary conditions independent equations order n.

title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: Let P_0 denote incident pressure amplitude. plane wave propagating along polar axis expanded : p_{1,\text{inc}}(r,\theta) =     P_0 e^{ik_1r\cos\theta} =     P_0\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(2n+1)^n \text{j}_n(k_1r)P_n(\cos\theta). use \text{j}_n spherical analogue using regular interior radial basis modal solutions. title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: exterior scattered field must satisfy Sommerfeld radiation condition therefore expanded outgoing spherical Hankel functions: p_{1,\text{scat}}(r,\theta) =     P_0\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(2n+1)^n A_n h_n^{(1)}(k_1r)P_n(\cos\theta). coefficients A_n determined boundary conditions.

title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: Using large-argument asymptotic form: h_n^{(1)}(kr) \sim \frac{(-)^{n+1}e^{ikr}}{kr},   \qquad r \\infty, scattered field becomes: p_{1,\text{scat}}(r,\theta) \sim   P_0\frac{e^{ik_1r}}{r}   \left[-\frac{}{k_1}   \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(2n+1)A_nP_n(\cos\theta)   \right]. backscattering direction \theta = \pi, one P_n(-1) = (-1)^n, backscattering amplitude : \mathcal{f}_\text{bs} = -\frac{}{k_1}   \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^n(2n+1)A_n.

title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: rigid sphere radius , normal fluid velocity vanishes surface. Since particle velocity proportional normal derivative pressure, boundary condition : \left.\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(p_{1,\text{inc}}+p_{1,\text{scat}})\right|_{r=} = 0. Substituting modal expansions using orthogonality gives, order n: \text{j}_n^\prime(k_1a) + A_n h_n^{(1)\,\prime}(k_1a) = 0. boundary condition gives scattered partial-wave coefficient: A_n = -\frac{\text{j}_n^\prime(k_1a)}{h_n^{(1)\,\prime}(k_1a)}.

Substitution modal expansions gives, mode mode: \text{j}_n(k_1a) + A_n h_n^{(1)}(k_1a) = 0. boundary condition gives scattered partial-wave coefficient: A_n = -\frac{\text{j}_n(k_1a)}{h_n^{(1)}(k_1a)}.

title: Spherical modal series solution (SPHMS) theory
text: fluid gas interior, pressure inside sphere must remain finite origin, interior field expanded regular spherical Bessel functions. Writing interior density wavenumber \rho_2 k_2, interior field becomes: p_2(r,\theta) = P_0\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(2n+1)^n B_n \text{j}_n(k_2r)P_n(\cos\theta). r = , pressure normal velocity continuous. region-indexed notation, pressure condition : p_1 = p_2, corresponding normal-velocity condition : \frac{1}{\rho_1}\frac{\partial p_1}{\partial r} =     \frac{1}{\rho_2}\frac{\partial p_2}{\partial r}. Since exterior total pressure p_1 = p_{1,\text{inc}} + p_{1,\text{scat}}, substituting modal expansions gives, order n: \begin{align*}     \text{j}_n(k_1a) + A_n h_n^{(1)}(k_1a) &= B_n \text{j}_n(k_2a), \\     \frac{k_1}{\rho_1}\left[\text{j}_n^\prime(k_1a) + A_n h_n^{(1)\,\prime}(k_1a)\right] &=       \frac{k_2}{\rho_2}B_n \text{j}_n^\prime(k_2a).   \end{align*} Solving pressure-continuity equation interior coefficient gives: B_n = \frac{\text{j}_n(k_1 ) + A_n h_n^{(1)}(k_1 )}   {\text{j}_n(k_2 )}. Substituting result velocity condition yields: \text{j}_n^\prime(k_1 ) + A_n h_n^{(1)\,\prime}(k_1 ) =     \frac{\rho_1 k_2}{\rho_2 k_1}   \frac{\text{j}_n^\prime(k_2 )}{\text{j}_n(k_2 )}   \left[\text{j}_n(k_1 ) + A_n h_n^{(1)}(k_1 )\right] Now define density sound-speed contrasts : g_{21} = \frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1},   \qquad   h_{21} = \frac{c_2}{c_1} k_2/k_1 = 1/h_{21}, prefactor \rho_1 k_2 / (\rho_2 k_1) becomes 1/(g_{21}h_{21}). Rearranging continuity equations therefore gives: A_n   \left[   h_n^{(1)\,\prime}(k_1 ) -\frac{1}{g_{21}h_{21}}   \frac{\text{j}_n'(k_2 )}{\text{j}_n(k_2 )}   h_n^{(1)}(k_1 )   \right] = \\     \frac{1}{g_{21}h_{21}}   \frac{\text{j}_n'(k_2 )}{\text{j}_n(k_2 )}   \text{j}_n(k_1 ) -\text{j}_n'(k_1 ) scattered partial-wave coefficient can therefore written explicitly : A_n =     \frac{   \dfrac{1}{g_{21}h_{21}}\dfrac{\text{j}_n^\prime(k_2 )}{\text{j}_n(k_2 )}\text{j}_n(k_1 ) -     \text{j}_n^\prime(k_1 )   }{   h_n^{(1)\,\prime}(k_1 ) -     \dfrac{1}{g_{21}h_{21}}\dfrac{\text{j}_n^\prime(k_2 )}{\text{j}_n(k_2 )}h_n^{(1)}(k_1 )   } direct spherical analogue fluid-filled cylindrical coefficient formulas seen modal models. important mathematical step elimination interior amplitude 2 \times 2 continuity system. title: Two-ray cylinder model (TRCM) theory
text: Let medium 1 denote surrounding fluid medium 2 denote cylinder interior. Let \rho_1 c_1 exterior density sound speed, let \rho_2 c_2 corresponding interior quantities. Define density sound-speed contrasts : g_{21} = \frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1},   \qquad   h_{21} = \frac{c_2}{c_1}, define corresponding acoustic impedances : Z_1 = \rho_1 c_1,   \qquad   Z_2 = \rho_2 c_2. exterior interior wavenumbers : k_1 = \frac{\omega}{c_1},   \qquad   k_2 = \frac{\omega}{c_2}, \omega angular frequency. normal incidence fluid-fluid interface, pressure reflection coefficient : \mathcal{R}_{12} =     \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} =     \frac{g_{21} h_{21} - 1}{g_{21} h_{21} + 1}. coefficient controls prompt reflected contribution. associated pressure-transmission coefficients entry exit cylinder : \mathcal{T}_{12} = 1 + \mathcal{R}_{12},   \qquad   \mathcal{T}_{21} = 1 - \mathcal{R}_{12}, product : \mathcal{T}_{12} \mathcal{T}_{21} =     1 - \mathcal{R}_{12}^2. product weights path enters body, traverses , exits .

title: Estimating Acoustic Target Strength via Physics-Based Scattering Models
text: acousticTS R package estimating acoustic target strength (TS) aquatic organisms objects using physics-based scattering models. supports range scatterer types: fluid-like bodies (e.g., fish, zooplankton), gas-filled bodies (e.g. swimbladders), elastic shells (e.g. pteropods, euphausiids), calibration spheres. provides unified interface parameterizing, running, comparing models across frequencies, orientations, morphologies. Acoustic backscatter single target expressed backscattering cross-section (\sigma_\text{bs}, m²). Target strength (TS, dB re. 1 m²) logarithmic form: TS = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma_\text{bs}) TS used : Convert integrated backscatter (e.g. S_\text{}), NASC) volumetric backscatter (S_\text{V}) number density biomass Classify backscatter species taxon based multi-frequency response Parameterize evaluate physics-based scattering models statistical distributions organism size orientation

title: Estimating Acoustic Target Strength via Physics-Based Scattering Models
text: Install latest release GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("brandynlucca/acousticTS")

title: Estimating Acoustic Target Strength via Physics-Based Scattering Models
text: acousticTS organizes targets five S4 classes: class stores shape (position matrix + morphometrics), body material properties (density sound speed contrasts), model results structured S4 object.

title: Estimating Acoustic Target Strength via Physics-Based Scattering Models
code: library(acousticTS)  # Build a fluid-like scatterer (e.g. krill) with a cylinder shape krill_shape <- cylinder(   length_body = 0.03,          # 30 mm body length   radius_body = 0.003          # 3 mm max radius ) # ---- Create the actual Scatterer-class object (FLS) krill <- fls_generate(   shape = krill_shape,   g = 1.0357,             # density contrast   h = 1.0279,             # sound speed contrast   theta = pi / 2          # broadside incidence )  # Run the DWBA model from 1 kHz to 300 kHz krill <- target_strength(krill,   frequency = seq(1e3, 300e3, by = 1e3),   model = "DWBA" )  # Plot TS vs frequency plot(krill, type = "model")

title: Estimating Acoustic Target Strength via Physics-Based Scattering Models
text: Scatterer shapes created via dedicated constructors: Existing scatterer objects can resized re-discretized without reconstructing scratch using reforge():
code: # Cylinder (used by FLS / CAL) fish_shape <- cylinder(length_body = 0.25, radius_body = 0.02)  # Prolate spheroid (used by FLS / PSMS) ps_shape <- prolate_spheroid(length_body = 0.02, radius_body = 0.002)  # Arbitrary shape from digitized position matrix arb_shape <- arbitrary(rpos = my_matrix)  # Dorsal/ventral style inputs arbitrary(   x_body = c(0, 0.015),   w_body = c(0.005, 0.0075),   zU_body = c(0.001, 0.002),   zL_body = c(-0.001, -0.002) ) # Rescale a krill to 40 mm krill_40mm <- reforge(krill, body_target = c(length = 0.04))  # Change segment count krill_fine <- reforge(krill, n_segments_body = 200)

title: Estimating Acoustic Target Strength via Physics-Based Scattering Models
text: simulate_ts() runs repeated model evaluations across distributions input parameters, supporting vectorized batch modes:
code: results <- simulate_ts(   krill,   model = "DWBA",   frequency = 120e3,   n_realizations = 1000,   parameters = list(     theta_body = function() rnorm(1, pi / 2, pi / 18),     length_body = function() rnorm(1, 0.03, 0.003)   ) )

title: Distorted wave Born approximation (DWBA) for weak scatterers — DWBA
text: DWBA approach derived weak scattering assumption, meaning differences compressibility (\(\kappa\)) density (\(\rho\)) scatterer surrounding fluid sufficiently small linearize acoustic scattering problem. linearization allows scattered field expressed integral scatterer volume. far-field backscattering amplitude given : $$    f_{bs} = \frac{k_1}{4\pi} \int \int \int\limits_v    (\gamma_\kappa - \gamma_\rho)    e^{2i k_2 \cdot r_v} dv  $$ $$    \gamma_\kappa = \frac{\kappa_2 - \kappa_1}{\kappa_1}, \quad    \gamma_\rho = \frac{\rho_2 - \rho_1}{\rho_2},  $$ $$    \kappa = (\rho c^2)^{-1}.  $$ \(c\) sound speed (m s-1). elongated bodies, volume integral reduces line intergral along body axis: $$    f_{bs} = \frac{k_1}{4} \int_{r_{pos}} (\gamma_\kappa - \gamma_\rho)    e^{2i k_2 \cdot r_{pos}}    \frac{\text{J}_1(2 k_2 \cos \beta_{tilt})}{\cos \beta_{tilt}}    |dr_{pos}|,  $$ \(\) local radius \(\beta_{tilt}\) local tilt angle. cylindrical Bessel function first kind order 1, \(\text{J}_1\), accounts circular cross-section segment. wavenumber \(k_2\) evaluated inside scatterer. Assumptions DWBA assumes scatterer weakly inhomogenous material property contrasts interior (\(c_2\), \(\rho_2\)) surrounding fluid (\(c_1\), \(\rho_1\)) : $$    g = \frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} \approx 1, \quad    h = \frac{c_2}{c_1} \approx 1.  $$ practice, \(c_2\) \(\rho_2\) within 5% \(c_1\) \(\rho_1\), respectively, can considered sufficent weak scattering assumption whereby: $$    |h - 1| \le 0.05, \quad |g - 1| \le 0.05.  $$ model also assumes scatterer's body sharp edges irregularities, cross-section symmetric around longitudinal axis. allows scattering integral reduced line integral along body axis first place, simplifying computation phase contributions different segments. Moreover, enables body discretized along-axis segments can approximate arbitrary body shapes. Since body axisymmetric smooth, allows DWBA applied arbitrary orientation angles without additional correction terms. DWBA provides first-order approximation neglects multiple scattering within body whereby secondary interactions different parts body ignored. valid weakly scattering objects amplitude scattered waves small consistent Born approximation wave physics. model also disregards elastic shelled effects, treating scatterers purely fluid-like. Consequently, lack internal elasticity means support shear waves resonances due solid boundaries. Mathematically, means contrasts compressibility density scatterer surrounding medium contribute scattered field.

title: Finite cylinder modal series (FCMS) solution — FCMS
text: modal series solution finite cylinder expresses backscattering amplitude : $$   f_{bs} = -\frac{L}{\pi} \frac{\sin(k L \cos \theta)}{k L \cos \theta}   \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} ^{m+1} B_m $$ \(L\) cylinder length, \(k\) wavenumber surrounding medium, \(\theta\) angle cylinder axis incident wave direction. coefficients \(B_m\) depend boundary condition cylinder surface. Boundary Conditions Modal Coefficients Rigid (fixed) cylinder: normal velocity surface zero. modal coefficient : $$     B_m = (-1)^m \epsilon_m \frac{J_m'(K )}{H_m^{(1)'}(K )}   $$ \(J_m\) \(H_m^{(1)}\) cylindrical Bessel Hankel functions order \(m\), prime denotes differentiation respect argument, \(K = k \sin \theta\), \(\) cylinder radius. Neumann factor \(\epsilon_0 = 1\), \(\epsilon_m = 2\) \(m \geq 1\). Pressure-release cylinder: acoustic pressure surface zero. modal coefficient : $$     B_m = (-1)^m \epsilon_m \frac{J_m(K )}{H_m^{(1)}(K )}   $$ Fluid-filled (gas-filled) cylinder: pressure normal velocity nonzero surface. modal coefficient : $$     B_m = -\epsilon_m / (1 + C_m)   $$ $$     C_m = \frac{       \left[ J_m'(K' ) Y_m(K ) \right] / \left[ J_m(K' ) J_m'(K ) \right]       - g h \left[ Y_m'(K ) / J_m'(K ) \right]     }{       \left[ J_m'(K' ) J_m(K ) \right] / \left[ J_m(K' ) J_m'(K ) \right]       - g h     }   $$ , \(Y_m\) cylindrical Bessel function second kind, \(K' = K / h\), \(g\) density contrast (target medium), \(h\) sound speed contrast (target medium). Modal Truncation modal sum truncated maximum order determined \(m_{\max} = \max(\lceil k \rceil) + 10\), sufficient convergence practical cases.

Stanton (1989) high-pass model generalizes approach spheres, prolate spheroids, cylinders, incorporates empirical terms account nulls scattering pattern deviations due shape complexity. sphere, Stanton formulation $$    \sigma_{bs} = \frac{      ^2 (ka)^4 \alpha_{\pi s}^2 \mathcal{G}    }{      1 + \frac{4(ka)^4 \alpha_{\pi s}^2}{\mathcal{R}^2 \mathcal{F}}    }  $$ \(\mathcal{G}\) null function accounts reductions scattering amplitude certain frequencies, \(\mathcal{F}\) deviation function accounts phase variability. prolate spheroid, Stanton formulation $$    \sigma_{bs} = \frac{      \frac{1}{9} L^2 (ka)^4 \alpha_{\pi c}^2 \mathcal{G}    }{      1 + \frac{\frac{16}{9}(ka)^4 \alpha_{\pi c}^2}      {\mathcal{R}^2 \mathcal{F}}    }  $$ \(L\) length prolate spheroid. straight cylinder angle \(\theta\), Stanton formulation $$    \sigma_{bs} = \frac{      \frac{1}{4} L^2 (Ka)^4 \alpha_{\pi c}^2 s^2 \mathcal{G}    }{      1 + \frac{\pi (Ka)^3 \alpha_{\pi c}^2}{\mathcal{R}^2 \mathcal{F}}    }  $$ \(K = k \sin \theta\), \(\) cylindrical radius, \(s = \sin(kL \cos \theta) / (kL \cos \theta)\) accounts finite length orientation cylinder. bent cylinder radius curvature \(\rho_c\) relative cylinder length \(L\), Stanton formulation $$  \sigma_{bs} = \frac{      \frac{1}{4} L^2 (ka)^4 \alpha_{\pi c}^2 \mathcal{H}^2 \mathcal{G}    }{      1 + \frac{        L^2 (ka)^4 \alpha_{\pi c}^2 \mathcal{H}^2      }{        \rho_c \mathcal{R}^2 \mathcal{F}      }    }  $$ \(\mathcal{H} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} (\rho_c / L) \sin(L / \rho_c)\) effective length factor accounts curvature. empirical functions \(\mathcal{F}\) \(\mathcal{G}\) specified user functions \(ka\), can used fit model measured numerically simulated scattering data. cases, wavenumber satisfies \(kr \gg 1\) \(L \ll 2\sqrt{r \lambda}\), \(r\)  distance scatterer receiver \(\lambda\) acoustic wavelength, ensuring far-field elongated object assumptions valid.

KRM model widely used fisheries acoustics estimating target strength, particularly fish gas-filled swimbladders. model represents fish body swimbladder series contiguous cylindrical elements computes coherent sum scattered fields. Depending frequency element size, scattering segment evaluated using either low-frequency modal solution high-frequency Kirchhoff (ray-based) approximation.

Effectively, \(\phi\) \(\theta\) correspond roll tilt angles (relative incident sound wave), respectively. assumed \(\theta'\) \(\phi'\) perpendicular incident wave : $$   \theta' = \pi - \theta \\   \phi' = \pi + \phi $$ pressure release (soft) rigid spheroids: $$   A_{mn} = \frac{-\Delta R_{mn}^{(1)}(h_0, \xi_0)}{   \Delta R_{mn}^{(3)}(h_0, \xi_0)   } $$ \(\Delta = 1\) soft \(\Delta = \partial / \partial \xi\) f rigid spheroid, \(R_{mn}^{()}\) radial spheroidal wave function -th kind. case fluid-filled spheroid, following simultaneous equation must solved: $$   \sum\limits_{n'=m}^\infty K_{nn'}^{(3)} A_{mn'} + \sum\limits_{n'=m}^\infty   K_{nn'}^{(1)} \alpha_{mn'}^{m} E_{n'}^{m(1)} = 0 $$ $$   K_{nl}^{()} = \frac{1}{N_{mn}(h_0)}   \int\limits_{-1}^{1} S_{mn}(h_0, \cos\theta)   S_{ml}(h_1, \eta) d\eta $$ $$   \alpha_{mn}^{m} = \frac{1}{N_{mn}(h_1)}   \int\limits_{-1}^{1} S_{mn}(h_0, \eta)   S_{mn}(h_1, \eta) d\eta $$ practice, kernel matrices solved numerically determine \(A_{mn}\). implementation uses compiled dense linear algebra keeps fluid-filled solve requested arithmetic linear-system stage become detached rest chosen precision pathway. case \(h_0 \approx h_1\), \(\alpha_{mn}^{m} \approx 0\) \(n \neq l\), much simplified expression derived: $$   A_{mn} = -\frac{E_{mn}^{m(1)}}{E_{mn}^{m(3)}} $$ maximum values \(m\) \(n\) can estimated : $$   m_{\max} = [2 k_0 b] \\   n_{\max} = m_{\max} + [h_0 / 2] $$

title: Prolate Spheroidal Radial Functions — Rmn
text: m Non-negative integer. order spheroidal function (\(m \geq 0\)). n Non-negative integer. degree spheroidal function (\(n \geq m\)). c Numeric. scalar size parameter (also denoted \(\gamma\) references). xi Numeric. radial coordinate evaluate function. Must satisfy \(\xi \geq 1\). kind Integer. Specifies kind radial function compute: 1 (first kind), 2 (second kind), 3 (third kind/outgoing), 4 (fourth kind/incoming). Default 1. precision Character. Either "double" (default) "quad". Controls  floating-point precision used underlying Fortran computation. "double" Uses standard double-precision (64-bit) arithmetic. Fastest  sufficient applications. "quad" Uses quadruple-precision (128-bit) arithmetic higher numerical  accuracy challenging parameter regimes (e.g., large \(m\), \(n\), \(c\), near  singularities). Computation significantly slower.

title: Prolate Spheroidal Radial Functions — Rmn

title: Prolate Spheroidal Radial Functions — Rmn
text: prolate spheroidal radial functions solutions radial part scalar Helmholtz equation prolate spheroidal coordinates. satisfy differential equation: $$(\xi^2 - 1) \frac{d^2 R_{mn}}{d\xi^2} + 2\xi \frac{dR_{mn}}{d\xi} - \left(\lambda_{mn} - c^2 \xi^2 + \frac{m^2}{\xi^2 - 1}\right) R_{mn} = 0$$ \(\lambda_{mn}\) separation constant (eigenvalue). Function kinds: kind = 1: Radial function first kind \(R_{mn}^{(1)}(c, \xi)\). Regular origin; analogous spherical Bessel functions \(j_n\). kind = 2: Radial function second kind \(R_{mn}^{(2)}(c, \xi)\). Singular focal points (\(\xi = 1\)); analogous spherical Neumann functions \(y_n\). kind = 3: Radial function third kind (outgoing Hankel-type) \(R_{mn}^{(3)}(c, \xi) = R_{mn}^{(1)}(c, \xi) +   R_{mn}^{(2)}(c, \xi)\). Used outgoing wave solutions. kind = 4: Radial function fourth kind (incoming Hankel-type) \(R_{mn}^{(4)}(c, \xi) = R_{mn}^{(1)}(c, \xi) -   R_{mn}^{(2)}(c, \xi)\). Used incoming wave solutions. Domain restrictions: radial coordinate must satisfy \(\xi \geq 1\) (prolate domain). order must satisfy \(m \geq 0\). degree must satisfy \(n \geq m\). Normalization: functions use Morse-Feshbach normalization default, radial functions reduce spherical Bessel/Neumann functions \(c \rightarrow 0\). Implementation: function R wrapper compiled C++ interface (Rmn_cpp), wraps Fortran subroutine profcn prolate_swf library developed Arnie Lee Van Buren Jeffrey Boisvert. underlying algorithm uses combination forward backward recursion Bouwkamp eigenvalue method high accuracy across wide parameter ranges. C++ layer manages memory, precision selection, data conversion R Fortran robust efficient computation.

Methods Theoretical Physics. McGraw-Hill, New York. Chapter 21. Flammer, C. (1957). Spheroidal Wave Functions. Stanford University Press. NIST Digital Library Mathematical Functions. title: Stochastic distorted wave Born approximation (SDWBA) for weak scatterers — SDWBA
text: SDWBA derived weak scattering assumption, differences compressibility (\(\kappa\)) density (\(\rho\)) scatterer surrounding fluid small enough linearize acoustic scattering problem (see DWBA). regime, multiple scattering within body neglected, total scattered field approximated coherent sum first-order contributions individual body segments. key extension introduced SDWBA inclusion stochastic phase variability represent unresolved morphological complexity, internal inhomogeneity, dynamic effects body flexure orientation variability. linear scattering coefficient written : $$    f_{bs}(\theta) = \sum\limits_{j=1}^N f_{bs}^{(j)}(\theta)    \exp(\varphi_j),  $$ \(N\) number body segments, \(f_{bs}^{(j)}\) contribution segment \(j\), \(\varphi_j\) random phase perturbation drawn independently segment. phase perturbations assumed follow zero-mean Gaussian distribution variance related effective signal--noise ratio (SNR) scattering process. minimum expected phase variance due noise given : $$    \mathbb{V}(\varphi_j) = \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{SNR}},  $$ though practice larger variances used account additional physical sources phase decorrelation explicitly modeled. expected backscattering cross-section obtained ensemble averaging multiple stochastic realizations: $$    \langle \sigma_{bs}(\theta) \rangle    = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \left| f_{bs}(\theta) \right|^2 \right]    \approx \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M}    \left| f_{bs}^{(m)}(\theta) \right|^2,  $$ expected target strength, \(\mathbb{E}[TS(\theta)]\), computed mean. ensure consistency across frequencies body sizes, SDWBA enforces scale invariance preserving product phase standard deviation, \(\mathrm{sd}_\varphi\), frequency, \(f\): $$    \mathrm{sd}_{\varphi}(f)\, f =    \mathrm{sd}_{\varphi_0}\, f_0,  $$ scaling number segments maintain constant spatial resolution relative acoustic wavelength: $$    N(f, L) = N_0 \frac{f L}{f_0 L_0}.  $$ phase standard deviation arbitrary frequency length : $$    \mathrm{sd}_{\varphi}(f, L) =    \mathrm{sd}_{\varphi_0}    \frac{N_0 L}{N(f, L) L_0}.  $$ scaling relationships ensure stochastic decorrelation effects remain physically consistent across different acoustic geometric regimes.

title: Solid elastic (calibration) sphere modal series (SOEMS) solution — SOEMS
text: calibration sphere model computes acoustic scattering solid elastic sphere expanding incident scattered fields terms spherical Bessel Hankel functions Legendre polynomials. compressional shear waves within sphere included, appropriate boundary conditions enforced sphere-water interface. dimensionless frequency parameter defined \(q = ka\), \(k\) wavenumber water \(\) sphere radius. longitudinal transverse wave numbers inside sphere $$    q_1 = \frac{qc}{c_1} \\    q_2 = \frac{qc}{c_2},  $$ \(c_1\) \(c_2\) longitudinal transverse sound speeds sphere, respectively. far-field backscattering form function given : $$    f_\infty(q) = -\frac{2}{q} \sum\limits_{\ell=0}^{\infty}    (-1)^\ell (2\ell+1)    \sin \eta_\ell \exp(\eta_\ell)  $$ phase angle mode given : $$    \tan \eta_\ell = -\frac{B_2 j_\ell'(q) - B_1 j_\ell(q)}{B_2 y_\ell'(q) -    B_1 y_\ell(q)}  $$ phase angle \(\eta_\ell\) determined boundary conditions material properties sphere surrounding fluid. phase angle \(\eta_\ell\) calculated using series coefficients. auxilliary quantities used determining boundary conditions subsequently \(\eta_\ell\) reported MacLennan (1981).

title: Prolate Spheroidal Angular Function of the First Kind, \(S^{1}_{mn}(c, \eta)\) — Smn
text: Computes prolate spheroidal angular function first kind, \(S_{mn}^{(1)}(c, \eta)\), first derivative respect \(\eta\) given order \(m\), degree \(n\), size parameter \(c\), angular coordinate \(\eta\). function R wrapper compiled C++ code, turn calls underlying Fortran library (prolate_swf) high-performance numerical computation. heavy computation performed compiled code speed accuracy.

title: Prolate Spheroidal Angular Function of the First Kind, \(S^{1}_{mn}(c, \eta)\) — Smn
code: Smn(m, n, c, eta, normalize = FALSE, precision = "double")

title: Prolate Spheroidal Angular Function of the First Kind, \(S^{1}_{mn}(c, \eta)\) — Smn
text: m Non-negative integer. order spheroidal function (\(m \geq 0\)). n Non-negative integer. degree spheroidal function (\(n \geq m\)). c Numeric. scalar size parameter. eta Numeric vector. angular coordinate(s) evaluate function. Must satisfy \(|\eta| \leq 1\). normalize Logical. TRUE, angular functions normalized unity norm. FALSE (default), Meixner-Schäfke normalization used. precision Character. Either "double" (default) "quad". Controls  floating-point precision used underlying Fortran computation. "double" Uses standard double-precision (64-bit) arithmetic. Fastest  sufficient applications. "quad" Uses quadruple-precision (128-bit) arithmetic higher numerical  accuracy challenging parameter regimes (e.g., large \(m\), \(n\), near  singularities). Computation significantly slower.

title: Prolate Spheroidal Angular Function of the First Kind, \(S^{1}_{mn}(c, \eta)\) — Smn
text: prolate spheroidal angular functions solutions angular part scalar Helmholtz equation prolate spheroidal coordinates. satisfy differential equation: $$(1 - \eta^2) \frac{d^2 S_{mn}}{d\eta^2} - 2\eta \frac{dS_{mn}}{d\eta} + \left(\lambda_{mn} - c^2 \eta^2 + \frac{m^2}{1 - \eta^2}\right) S_{mn} = 0$$ \(\lambda_{mn}\) separation constant (eigenvalue). Domain restrictions: angular coordinate must satisfy \(|\eta| \leq 1\). order must satisfy \(m \geq 0\). degree must satisfy \(n \geq m\). Normalization: normalize = FALSE (default), functions use Meixner-Schäfke normalization, matches normalization corresponding associated Legendre functions. normalize = TRUE, functions scaled unity norm. Implementation: function R wrapper compiled C++ interface (Smn_cpp), wraps Fortran subroutine profcn prolate_swf library developed Arnie Lee Van Buren Jeffrey Boisvert. underlying algorithm uses combination forward backward recursion Bouwkamp eigenvalue method high accuracy across wide parameter ranges. C++ layer manages memory, precision selection, data conversion R Fortran robust efficient computation.

title: Generate a BBF-class object. — bbf_generate

code: body_shape <- arbitrary(   x_body = c(0, 0.04, 0.08),   zU_body = c(0.001, 0.004, 0.001),   zL_body = c(-0.001, -0.004, -0.001) ) backbone_shape <- cylinder(   length_body = 0.06,   radius_body = 0.0008,   n_segments = 40 ) bbf_generate(   body_shape = body_shape,   backbone_shape = backbone_shape,   density_body = 1070,   sound_speed_body = 1570,   density_backbone = 1900,   sound_speed_longitudinal_backbone = 3500,   sound_speed_transversal_backbone = 1700 ) #> BBF-object #>  Backboned fish (BBF)  #>  ID:UID #> Body dimensions: #>  Length:0.08 m(n = 2 segments) #>  Mean radius:0.002 m #>  Max radius:0.004 m #> Backbone dimensions: #>  Length:0.06 m(n = 40 segments) #>  Mean radius:8e-04 m #>  Max radius:8e-04 m #> Body material properties: #>  Density: 1070 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 1570 m s^-1 #> Backbone elastic properties: #>  Density: 1900 kg m^-3 | cL: 3500 m s^-1 | cT: 1700 m s^-1 #> Body orientation:1.571 radians | Backbone orientation:1.571 radians

title: Calculate the bulk modulus (K). — bulk
text:
Calculates bulk modulus (K) two three elastic moduli: Young's modulus (E), shear modulus (G), Poisson's ratio (\(
u\)). Assumes 3D material properties. relationships used : $$K =  rac{E G}{3(3G - E)}$$ $$K =  rac{2G(1 + 
u)}{3(1 - 2
u)}$$ $$K =  rac{E}{3(1 - 2
u)}$$

code: canonicalize_shape(   shape,   to = c("ProlateSpheroid", "Cylinder", "Sphere", "OblateSpheroid"),   method = c("auto", "volume", "length_volume", "profile_l2"),   n_segments = NULL,   diagnostics = FALSE )

title: Canonicalize one shape into a canonical surrogate — canonicalize_shape
text: shape Shape object approximate. Canonical target shape. One "Sphere", "Cylinder", "ProlateSpheroid", "OblateSpheroid". method Canonicalization rule. "auto" selects shape-specific default: "volume" spheres, "length_volume" cylinders, "profile_l2" spheroids. "volume" preserves enclosed volume . "length_volume" preserves body length enclosed volume. "profile_l2" fits target canonical profile directly source equivalent-radius profile least squares. n_segments Optional number segments returned canonical shape. Defaults source shape segment count. diagnostics Logical. FALSE (default), return canonical Shape. TRUE, return list containing canonical shape fit diagnostics.

title: Canonicalize one shape into a canonical surrogate — canonicalize_shape
text: diagnostics = FALSE, canonical Shape object. diagnostics = TRUE, list elements: shape: fitted canonical Shape diagnostics: named list source, target, fit metrics

title: Canonicalize one shape into a canonical surrogate — canonicalize_shape
text: canonicalize_shape() intentionally explicit. run automatically inside model calls target_strength(..., model = "psms"), single defensible canonicalization rule every biological segmented target. input shape contains explicit width profile upper/lower height profiles, canonicalization step first reduces local cross-sections equal-area circular radius fitting canonical surrogate. keeps reduction axisymmetric still honoring original local cross-sectional area closely height-radius. returned diagnostics report source target compare length, enclosed volume, maximum equivalent radius, equivalent-radius profile root-mean-square error source x-grid. diagnostics package's recommended way judge whether canonical surrogate defensible intended model comparison.

title: Canonicalize one shape into a canonical surrogate — canonicalize_shape
code: bladder_like <- arbitrary(   x_body = c(0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04),   radius_body = c(0, 0.004, 0.006, 0.004, 0) )  psms_shape <- canonicalize_shape(   bladder_like,   to = "ProlateSpheroid" )  cyl_fit <- canonicalize_shape(   bladder_like,   to = "Cylinder",   diagnostics = TRUE ) cyl_fit$diagnostics$fit$radius_nrmse #> [1] 0.4567582

what: Calculate she compressibility (\(\kappa\)) of a scattering boundary/interface. — compressibility
title: Calculate she compressibility (\(\kappa\)) of a scattering boundary/interface. — compressibility
text: Calculates compressibility contrast (\(\kappa\)) scattering interface surrounding medium. Compressibility defined : $$  K = \frac{1}{\rho c^2} $$ \(\rho\) density (\(kg~m^{-3}\)) \(c\) sound speed (\(m~s^{-1}\)). compressibility contrast : $$  \kappa = \frac{K_2 - K_1}{K_1} $$ \(K_1\) compressibility medium \(K_2\) target interface.

title: Calculate she compressibility (\(\kappa\)) of a scattering boundary/interface. — compressibility
code: compressibility(medium, target)

title: Calculate she compressibility (\(\kappa\)) of a scattering boundary/interface. — compressibility
text: medium Dataframe object containing density (\(kgm^{-3}\)) sound speed (\(ms^{-1}\)) values fluid medium external scattering interface (e.g., seawater). target Dataframe object containing density (\(kgm^{-3}\)) sound speed (\(ms^{-1}\)) values target boundary.

title: Calculate she compressibility (\(\kappa\)) of a scattering boundary/interface. — compressibility

title: Generate a FLS-class object. — fls_generate
text: preferred workflow build geometry first sphere(), cylinder(), prolate_spheroid(), arbitrary(), pass Shape object fls_generate(). Material properties can supplied either contrasts (g_body/h_body) absolute density/sound-speed values (density_body/sound_speed_body), property pair. Downstream models derive contrasts automatically absolute values supplied. supported public geometry paths now: supply pre-built Shape object, supply explicit profile coordinates directly constructor. Character-based shape dispatch retained compatibility pathway now deprecated. Internally, every pathway resolved Shape-first geometry contract FLS object built. Scatterer constructors store geometry meters orientations radians. length_units theta_units retained compatibility arguments, non-SI values normalized package-standard representation.

title: Generate a FLS-class object. — fls_generate
code: shape <- prolate_spheroid(   length_body = 0.04, radius_body = 0.004, n_segments = 50 ) fls_generate(   shape = shape, density_body = 1045, sound_speed_body = 1520 ) #> FLS-object #>  Fluid-like scatterer  #>  ID:UID #> Body dimensions: #>  Length:0.04 m(n = 50 cylinders) #>  Mean radius:0.0031 m #>  Max radius:0.004 m #> Shape parameters: #>  Defined shape:ProlateSpheroid #>  L/a ratio:10 #>  Taper order:N/A #> Material properties: #>  Density: 1045 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 1520 m s^-1 #> Body orientation (relative to transducer face/axis):1.571 radians

title: Generate a GAS-class object — gas_generate
text: shape Pre-built Shape object describing gas-filled geometry. omitted, explicit profile coordinates x_body, y_body, z_body treated manual geometry pathway. Legacy character dispatch "sphere" retained backward compatibility now deprecated. radius_body Radius (m). non-canonical shapes, maximum mean radius scatterer midsection. h_fluid Sound speed contrast fluid relative surrounding medium (h). g_fluid Density contrast fluid relative surrounding density (g). sound_speed_fluid Optional fluid sound speed (m/s). density_fluid Optional fluid density (m/s). theta_body Orientation target relative incident wave (radians). ID Optional metadata identifier. radius_units Compatibility argument. gas_generate() now assumes meters ignores non-SI alternatives. theta_units Compatibility argument. Scatterer constructors now assume radians ignore non-SI alternatives. n_segments Number body segments. ... Additional manual profile arguments legacy canonical shape arguments used compatibility geometry pathway, x_body, y_body, z_body, radius_body, length_body, taper.

title: Generate a GAS-class object — gas_generate
text: preferred workflow supply pre-built Shape object explicit profile coordinates describe internal gas either contrasts (g_fluid, h_fluid) absolute density/sound-speed values (density_fluid, sound_speed_fluid). Character-based shape dispatch remains available compatibility pathway now deprecated. Scatterer constructors store geometry meters orientations radians. radius_units theta_units retained compatibility arguments, non-SI values normalized package-standard representation.

code: shape_gas <- sphere(radius_body = 0.01, n_segments = 60) gas_generate(shape = shape_gas, g_fluid = 0.0012, h_fluid = 0.22) #> GAS-object #>  Gas- and fluid-filled scatterer  #>  ID:UID #> Body dimensions: #>  Diameter:0.02 m #>  Radius:0.01 m #> Material properties: #>  g: 0.0012 #>  h: 0.22

text: n Number quadrature nodes (n >= 1). Left endpoint integration interval. b Right endpoint integration interval (b > ).

title: Gauss-Legendre nodes and weights — gauss_legendre
text: list components: nodes Quadrature abscissae \(x_i\) \([,~b]\). weights Quadrature weights \(w_i\) \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx \approx \sum_{=1}^n w_i\,f(x_i).\)

title: Gauss-Legendre nodes and weights — gauss_legendre
text: Gauss-Legendre quadrature provides exact integration polynomials degree \(2n-1\) using n nodes weights chosen roots Legendre polynomial \(P_n(x)\) canonical interval \([-1,1]\). general interval \([,b]\) canonical nodes shifted rescaled onto \([,b]\), weights scaled \((b-)/2\). wrapper performs basic argument validation calls C++ routine obtain nodes weights high accuracy moderate n.

"cosine" gives smooth bump centered inside x_range, "linear" gives triangular bump, "box" applies uniform factor inside interval. containment Containment policy used moved swimbladder backbone checked body: "warn", "error", "ignore".

title: Calculate Lamé's first parameter (\(\lambda\)) — lame
text:
Calculates Lamé's first parameter (\(\lambda\)) two four elastic moduli: bulk modulus (K), Young's modulus (E), shear modulus (G), Poisson's ratio (\(
u\)). Assumes 3D material properties. relationships used : $$\lambda = K -  rac{2G}{3}$$ $$\lambda =  rac{E
u}{(1 + 
u)(1 - 2
u)}$$ $$\lambda =  rac{2G
u}{1 - 2
u}$$ $$\lambda =  rac{3K
u}{1 + 
u}$$ $$\lambda =  rac{3K(3K - E)}{9K - E}$$ $$\lambda =  rac{G(E - 2G)}{3G - E}$$

title: Calculate Lamé's first parameter (\(\lambda\)) — lame
code: lame(K = NULL, E = NULL, G = NULL, nu = NULL)

title: Calculate Lamé's first parameter (\(\lambda\)) — lame
text: K Bulk modulus (Pa). E Young's modulus (Pa). G Shear modulus (Pa). nu Poisson's ratio (Dimensionless).

title: Calculate Lamé's first parameter (\(\lambda\)) — lame
text: Lamé's first parameter (\(\lambda\), Pa).

what: Convert between logarithmic (dB) and linear domains for backscatter values. — linear
title: Convert between logarithmic (dB) and linear domains for backscatter values. — linear
text: linear function converts value logarithmic (dB) domain linear domain, db function converts value linear domain logarithmic (dB) domain. commonly used target strength (TS) backscattering coefficient (\(\sigma_{bs}\)) conversions. conversions defined : $$\text{linear}(x) = c^{x / c}$$ $$\text{db}(x) = c \log_c(x)$$ \(c\) coefficient (default 10).

title: Convert between logarithmic (dB) and linear domains for backscatter values. — linear
code: linear(value, coefficient = 10)  db(value, coefficient = 10)

title: Convert between logarithmic (dB) and linear domains for backscatter values. — linear

title: Convert between logarithmic (dB) and linear domains for backscatter values. — linear
text: linear, returns value converted linear domain. db, returns value converted logarithmic (dB) domain.

text: Neumann factor, denoted \(\nu_{n}\), simple multiplicative constant commonly used spherical spheroidal function theory. accounts symmetry cosine terms duplication even-order contributions integrals.

code: neumann(x)

title: Compute the Neumann factor \(\nu_{n}\) — neumann
text: x integer iterator.

title: Compute the Neumann factor \(\nu_{n}\) — neumann
text: numeric vector length x, containing values \(\eta_x\), equal 1 2.

title: Compute the Neumann factor \(\nu_{n}\) — neumann
text: Formally, Neumann factor defined : $$   \eta_n = \begin{cases}     1, & n = 0, \\     2, & n > 0.   \end{cases} $$ factor frequently appears normalization spherical Bessel functions, Legendre expansions, spheroidal wave functions. related "von Neumann ordinals" used set theory.

title: Compute the Neumann factor \(\nu_{n}\) — neumann
code: neumann(0) # should return 1 #> [1] 1 neumann(1) # should return 2 #> [1] 2 neumann(2) # should return 2 #> [1] 2  # Vectorized use: neumann(0:4) #> [1] 1 2 2 2 2

Use "shape" stored geometry, "model" currently stored model output, "scattering" stored TMM angular products. nudge_y Multiplicative vertical padding applied automatically derived y-axis limits. nudge_x Multiplicative horizontal padding applied automatically derived x-axis limits. aspect_ratio Aspect-ratio control supported shape plots. title: Calculate the Poisson's ratio (\(\nu\)) — pois
text:
Calculates Poisson's ratio (\(
u\)) two three elastic moduli: bulk modulus (K), Young's modulus (E), shear modulus (G). Assumes 3D material properties. relationships used : $$
u =  rac{E}{2G} - 1$$ $$
u =  rac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)}$$ $$
u =  rac{3K - E}{6K}$$

title: Creates a polynomial deformed cylinder — polynomial_cylinder
code: # We can use the polynomial coefficients defined in Smith et al. (2013) to # define the position vector of a sub-Arctic krill. poly_vec <- c(0.83, 0.36, -2.10, -1.20, 0.63, 0.82, 0.64) # Create the position vector # This outputs a list containing "rpos" and "radius" pos <- polynomial_cylinder(   length_body = 15e-3, radius_body = 2e-3,   polynomial = poly_vec ) str(pos) #> Formal class 'PolynomialCylinder' [package "acousticTS"] with 2 slots #>   ..@ position_matrix : num [1:101, 1:3] 0 0.00015 0.0003 0.00045 0.0006 ... #>   .. ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   .. .. ..$ : NULL #>   .. .. ..$ : chr [1:3] "x" "y" "z" #>   ..@ shape_parameters:List of 5 #>   .. ..$ length             : num 0.015 #>   .. ..$ radius             : num [1:101] 4.00e-05 8.75e-05 1.35e-04 1.83e-04 2.30e-04 ... #>   .. ..$ length_radius_ratio: num 7.85 #>   .. ..$ n_segments         : num 100 #>   .. ..$ length_units       : chr "m"

title: Generate a SBF-class object. — sbf_generate
text: x_body Vector containing along-body axis (m). w_body Vector containing across-body axis (m). zU_body Vector containing dorsal-body axis (m). zL_body Vector containing ventral-body axis (m). x_bladder Vector containing along-bladder axis (m). w_bladder Vector containing across-bladder axis (m). zU_bladder Vector containing dorsal-bladder axis (m). zL_bladder Vector containing ventral-bladder axis (m). sound_speed_body Flesh sound speed (c;body, m s-1). sound_speed_bladder Bladder sound speed (c, m \(s^-1\)). g_body Body density contrast. h_body Body sound speed contrast. g_bladder Bladder density contrast. h_bladder Bladder sound speed contrast. density_body Flesh density (ρbody, kg m3). density_bladder Bladder density (\(\rho\), kg m3). theta_body Angle body relative wavefront (\(\theta_body\), radians). theta_bladder Angle bladder relative wavefront (\(\theta_bladder\), radians). theta_units Compatibility argument. Scatterer constructors now assume radians ignore non-SI alternatives. length_units Compatibility argument. Scatterer constructors now assume meters ignore non-SI alternatives. ID Optional metadata identifier. body_shape Optional pre-built Shape body; overrides x_body/w_body/zU_body/zL_body supplied. bladder_shape Optional pre-built Shape bladder; overrides x_bladder/w_bladder/zU_bladder/zL_bladder supplied.

title: Generate a SBF-class object. — sbf_generate
text: recommended interface supply pre-built Shape objects body_shape bladder_shape, specify material properties either contrasts (g_*, h_*) absolute density/sound-speed values. Legacy coordinate-vector inputs retained backward compatibility converted internally Shape objects scatterer built. Character-based shape dispatch deprecated; build component geometry first Shape constructor pass resulting Shape object. Body/bladder width vectors (w_body, w_bladder) default zeros missing. Resulting rpos matrices always carry row names x_body, w_body, zU_body, zL_body body x_bladder, w_bladder, zU_bladder, zL_bladder bladder.

title: Generate a SBF-class object. — sbf_generate
code: # Manual body/bladder coordinates sbf_generate(   x_body = seq(0, 0.1, length.out = 5),   w_body = rep(0, 5),   zU_body = seq(0.001, 0.002, length.out = 5),   zL_body = -seq(0.001, 0.002, length.out = 5),   x_bladder = seq(0.02, 0.09, length.out = 4),   w_bladder = rep(0, 4),   zU_bladder = rep(0.0015, 4),   zL_bladder = rep(-0.0015, 4),   sound_speed_body = 1500,   sound_speed_bladder = 340,   density_body = 1040,   density_bladder = 1.2 ) #> SBF-object #>  Swimbladdered fish (SBF)  #>  ID:UID #> Body dimensions: #>  Length:0.1 m(n = 4 cylinders) #>  Mean radius:0.0015 m #>  Max radius:0.002 m #> Bladder dimensions: #>  Length:0.07 m(n = 3 cylinders) #>  Mean radius:0.0015 m #>  Max radius:0.0015 m #> Body material properties: #>  Density: 1040 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 1500 m s^-1 #> Bladder fluid material properties: #>  Density: 1.2 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 340 m s^-1 #> Body orientation (relative to transducer face/axis):1.571 radians  # Using pre-built shapes body_shape <- arbitrary(   x_body = c(0, 0.1), zU_body = c(0.001, 0.002),   zL_body = c(-0.001, -0.002) ) bladder_shape <- arbitrary(   x_bladder = c(0.02, 0.09), w_bladder = c(0, 0),   zU_bladder = c(0.0015, 0.0015),   zL_bladder = c(-0.0015, -0.0015) ) sbf_generate(   body_shape = body_shape, bladder_shape = bladder_shape,   sound_speed_body = 1500, sound_speed_bladder = 340,   density_body = 1040, density_bladder = 1.2 ) #> SBF-object #>  Swimbladdered fish (SBF)  #>  ID:UID #> Body dimensions: #>  Length:0.1 m(n = 1 cylinders) #>  Mean radius:0.0015 m #>  Max radius:0.002 m #> Bladder dimensions: #>  Length:0.07 m(n = 1 cylinders) #>  Mean radius:0.0015 m #>  Max radius:0.0015 m #> Body material properties: #>  Density: 1040 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 1500 m s^-1 #> Bladder fluid material properties: #>  Density: 1.2 kg m^-3 | Sound speed: 340 m s^-1 #> Body orientation (relative to transducer face/axis):1.571 radians

title: Calculate the shear modulus (G) — shear
text:
#' Calculates shear modulus (G) two three elastic moduli: bulk modulus (K), Young's modulus (E), Poisson's ratio (\(
u\)). Assumes 3D material properties. relationships used : $$G =  rac{3KE}{9K - E}$$ $$G =  rac{3K(1 - 2
u)}{2(1 + 
u)}$$ $$G =  rac{E}{2(1 + 
u)}$$

title: Wrapper function to model acoustic target strength — target_strength
text: object Scatterer-class object. frequency Frequency (Hz). model Model name. Available models currently include "dwba" (DWBA), "bbfm" (BBFM), "pcdwba" (PCDWBA), "sdwba" (SDWBA), "fcms" (FCMS), "bcms" (BCMS), "ecms" (ECMS), "hpa" (HPA), "krm" (KRM), "psms" (PSMS), "sphms" (SPHMS), "essms" (ESSMS), "vesms" (VESMS), "trcm" (TRCM), "calibration" / "soems" (SOEMS). verbose Prints current procedural step occurring model initialization calculating TS. Defaults FALSE. model_args Optional named list per-model argument bundles. list name match one requested model names case-insensitively, value either named list named atomic vector arguments apply model. argument supplied ... model_args[[model_name]], model-specific entry takes precedence. ... Additional optional model inputs/parameters.

title: Wrapper function to model acoustic target strength — target_strength
text: input scatterer object requested model parameters, model outputs, target strength results stored model slots.

title: Wrapper function to model acoustic target strength — target_strength
text: main high-level entry point running target-strength models acousticTS. supplied scatterer object checked, requested model models initialized, resulting outputs stored back object. available model families span exact modal-series solutions approximation-based solutions. Readers consult model-specific help topics physical assumptions, valid object types, boundary-condition options, model-specific arguments used implementation: DWBA distorted-wave Born approximation applied weakly scattering fluid-like bodies. BBFM composite flesh-plus-backbone model combines DWBA flesh term elastic-cylinder backbone term. PCDWBA phase-compensated distorted-wave Born approximation applied elongated fluid-like bodies. SDWBA stochastic distorted-wave Born approximation. FCMS finite cylinder modal series solution. BCMS bent-cylinder modal series solution. ECMS elastic-cylinder modal series solution. HPA high-pass approximation family. KRM Kirchhoff-Ray Mode model. PSMS prolate spheroidal modal series solution. SPHMS spherical modal series solution. ESSMS elastic-shelled spherical modal series solution. VESMS viscous-elastic spherical scattering model applied gas-filled elastic shells external viscous layer. TMM single-target transition-matrix family. TRCM two-ray cylindrical model. SOEMS solid elastic calibration-sphere model, accessed "calibration" "soems". Model-specific inputs usually passed .... example, models require boundary argument, HPA uses method argument, several models expose additional numerical controls. several models requested together, shared arguments may supplied ... per-model overrides may supplied model_args. useful different models share seawater properties one needs extra stochastic numerical control: legacy curved-entry wrappers "dwba_curved" "sdwba_curved" deprecated; apply brake scatterer first, run "dwba" "sdwba" curved object. Model names normalized internally, case-insensitive inputs "DWBA" "dwba" resolve family.
code: target_strength(   object,   frequency,   model = c("dwba", "sdwba"),   density_sw = 1026,   sound_speed_sw = 1478,   model_args = list(     sdwba = list(phase_sd_init = 0.77)   ) )

title: Calculate Young's modulus (E). — young
text:
Calculates Young's modulus (E) two three elastic moduli: bulk modulus (K), shear modulus (G), Poisson's ratio (\(
u\)). Assumes 3D material properties. relationships used : $$E =  rac{9KG}{3K + G}$$ $$E = 3K(1 - 2
u)$$ $$E = 2G(1 + 
u)$$

title: acousticTS 2026.3.0
text: DCM model fully deprecated due 1) misleading name 2) limited scope. superseded TRCM, uses adjusted implementation allows straight bent cylinder geometries. DWBA_curved SDWBA_curved models deprecated. now expected users explicitly apply brake shapes (.e., adds curvature) prior using standard DWBA SDWBA models. MSS_anderson model deprecated replaced SPHMS. high_pass_stanton model deprecated replaced HPA. MSS_goodman_stern model deprecated replaced ESSMS. Additional geometry class guardrails added across package. legacy workflows may now error supplied shape, scatterer class, boundary assumptions incompatible selected model.

title: acousticTS 2026.3.0
text: SPHMS: Modal-series solution canonical spherical targets across multiple supported boundary conditions. FCMS: Modal-series solution finite cylinders across multiple supported boundary conditions. PSMS: Modal-series solution prolate spheroids across multiple supported boundary conditions. SOEMS / calibration: solid elastic sphere workflow broadened documented general solid-elastic-sphere family rather calibration-sphere workflow. calibration remains available compatibility alias. ESSMS: Modal-series solution elastic-shelled spheres. BCMS: Modal-series solution finite, bent cylinders. ECMS: Modal-series solution solid elastic finite cylinders. KRM: Now incorporates breathing-mode modal-series calculation ka < 0.15 updated better align alternative body sound-speed assumptions. HPA: Expanded usable geometries now includes Johnson (1977) formulation. TRCM: two-ray cylinder model now supports straight bent cylinders. PCDWBA: Phase-compensated distorted-wave Born approximation bent weakly scattering targets. BBFM: Composite flesh-plus-backbone model swimbladder-less fish-like targets, treating backbone solid elastic scatterer. VESMS (VESM family): Viscous-elastic layered-sphere model gas-core, shell, viscous-layer targets. TMM: Transition-matrix framework monostatic angle-dependent scattering spheres, prolate spheroids, oblate spheroids, guarded finite-cylinder branches.

title: acousticTS 2026.3.0
text: Cylindrical spherical Bessel functions first, second, third kinds overhauled expanded, including broader support orders, purely imaginary arguments, public k^{\text{th}} derivative helpers jcdk(), hcdk(), jsdk(), ysdk(). Full public support prolate spheroidal angular radial wave functions via Smn() Rmn(), including derivatives. Public Legendre support now includes first second kinds, including Pn(), Pndk(), Qn(), Qndk(). New numerical helpers gauss_legendre() related modal/scattering utilities added support higher-level model implementations.

title: acousticTS 2026.3.0
text: Shape- scatterer-generation workflows substantially streamlined reorganized. simulate_ts() provides parameter-sweep repeated-realization workflows TS simulation. canonicalize_shape() converts arbitrary shapes canonical surrogates spheres, prolate spheroids, oblate spheroids, cylinders. reforge() supports resizing reparameterizing supported scatterer objects. extract() provides consistent way pull nested components parameters supported objects. available_models() register_model() unregister_model() reset_model_registry() tmm_scattering() tmm_scattering_grid() tmm_products() tmm_bistatic_summary() tmm_average_orientation() tmm_orientation_distribution() tmm_diagnostics()

title: acousticTS 2026.3.0
text: New elastic-property helpers added, including lame(), pois(), bulk(), young(), shear(). Core acoustic utilities wavenumber(), compressibility(), transmission_coefficient(), degrees(), radians() expanded cleaned . Example-data support improved, including bundled fish benchmark objects used throughout documentation validation workflows.

previous_headings: What’s Changed > New features and enhancements ✨
title: acousticTS 2026.3.0
text: formal validation benchmarking registry added track benchmarked, validated, partially validated, unvalidated, experimental model families. new benchmark_ts dataset added support canonical comparison ladders across modal-series families. Reproducible implementation-figure comparison pipelines added documented model families. Cross-software cross-reference validation coverage expanded across models SPHMS, FCMS, PSMS, SOEMS, KRM, HPA, PCDWBA, VESMS, TMM. pkgdown model library now exposes family-level validation badges supporting summaries directly site.

previous_headings: What’s Changed > New features and enhancements ✨
title: acousticTS 2026.3.0
text: Major compiled backends added C++ Fortran, substantially improving performance across special functions core models. affects Bessel functions, Legendre functions, spheroidal wave functions, DWBA / SDWBA, PSMS, ESSMS, TMM, VESMS. Adaptive methods added SOEMS / calibration, giving users control balance speed precision. Optional modal-truncation controls m_limit added expanded across modal-series solutions, allowing users trade precision speed explicitly higher ka.

text: package documentation fully reorganized pkgdown site model-family overview, theory, implementation pages. large new vignette set added, including package primers, model selection, numerical foundations, notation, material properties, boundary conditions, example data, shape/scatterer construction, running models, comparing models, plotting results, simulation sweeps, validation benchmarks, model creation workflows. Model-family landing pages model library added make package structure validation status easier navigate. Figure-generation vignette-rendering workflows standardized expanded support implementation validation documentation.

Numerous fixes applied across shape generation, scatterer handling, plotting, model dispatch, documentation rendering, pkgdown build behavior. Several numerical edge cases validation pathways tightened across special-function modal-series infrastructure.

text: automated test suite expanded substantially across models, special functions, geometry helpers, simulation workflows, validation utilities, pkgdown helpers. Additional validation-specific implementation-specific regression tests added support new model families compiled backends.

Package metadata release-support files improved, including NEWS.md CITATION.cff. Internal package organization significantly restructured, including model dispatch, utilities, plotting helpers, validation helpers, compiled source layout.
